titleStmt.title: Letter from Francis M. Shaw to Fr Provincial Thomas V. Nolan, 27 March 1918
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Francis M. Shaw

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p:
Letter from Francis M. Shaw to Father Provincial regarding economic issues.
Francis M. Shaw worked in the 16th General Field Hospital at Le Tréporte. He was ordained on 31st July 1916 at Milltown Park in Dublin and in 1917 was dispatched to India, and later to Mesopotamia, possibly due to his nationalistic views.
Fr. Thomas V. Nolan (1867-1941) was the Provincial of the Irish Province of the Society of Jesus (1912-1922) and was a member of the Distribution Committee which looked after the welfare and distribution of the the Belgian refugees who arrived in Ireland as a consequence of the First World War.

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
27 March 1918. 16 Casualty Clearing Sta.
Mesop. Exped. Force. Dear Fr Provincial,
P.C. Your Reverence's last letter came, with Ords, and
a notice ofthe death ofMr Carbery, just after I had posted
my last mail. The province seems tobe losing a
large number lately. Thanks very much for
the help you sent tomy sister's. I wrote to my eldest
brother Jack (he is in the National Bank, Ennistymon) to
tell him that they were not well off enough tobe able
to "carry on" with much comfort during these hard
times. I hope our chaplains in France will
be safe during this tremendous push that has just
started. The Lord will have to take special care of
some ofthem if they are tocome out fit. Sometimes out here I have to do battle ( I, a Jesuit)
in defence ofthe principle that wrong must not be done
the good may come ofit. It is wonderful what lose
thinking there is, and loser grasp of even fundamental
morality among some ofthe officers. I think the lose
thinking is almost universal. They cant see that if
German air-raids are wrong, the English retaliation
in kind is wrong also. Among the offenders, are
some four step-brothers ofthe Establishment. I am baptizing a Hindu on Easter Sunday, and there are
two or three more on the road. Your Reverence's sincerely in Christ, Francis M. Shaw S.J.
